stem: In right triangle ABC with the right angle at B, point D is the foot of the altitude from B to the hypotenuse AC. If angle A = 30 degrees and BD = 9, what is the length of AC?
explanation: In right triangle ABD (with angle ADB = 90 degrees), angle A = 30 degrees, so angle ABD = 60 degrees. Using BD = 9: AB = BD / sin(30) = 9 / (1/2) = 18, and AD = BD / tan(30) = 9 / (1/sqrt(3)) = 9sqrt(3). In right triangle BDC (with angle BDC = 90 degrees), angle C = 60 degrees (from the original triangle), so angle DBC = 30 degrees. Using BD = 9: DC = BD / tan(60) = 9 / sqrt(3) = 3sqrt(3). Therefore AC = AD + DC = 9sqrt(3) + 3sqrt(3) = 12sqrt(3). Verification: AB / AC = sin(60) gives 18 / (12sqrt(3)) = 18 / (12sqrt(3)) = 3 / (2sqrt(3)) = sqrt(3)/2 = sin(60). Confirmed.

distractorRationale.A: Correct: Finds AD = 9sqrt(3) from triangle ABD and DC = 3sqrt(3) from triangle BDC using the correct angles, then AC = AD + DC = 12sqrt(3)
distractorRationale.B: Uses angle 30 degrees in triangle BDC instead of the correct angle 60 degrees to compute DC: DC = BD / tan(30) = 9sqrt(3) instead of 3sqrt(3), giving AC = 9sqrt(3) + 9sqrt(3) = 18sqrt(3)
distractorRationale.C: Finds AB = BD / sin(30) = 18 in triangle ABD but reports AB as the hypotenuse AC
distractorRationale.D: Finds AD = 9sqrt(3) in triangle ABD but reports AD as the full hypotenuse AC, omitting the segment DC = 3sqrt(3)

imageDescription: Right triangle ABC with the right angle marked at B. Point D lies on hypotenuse AC, and a perpendicular line segment BD is drawn from B to AC, with a right angle mark at D. The angle at A is labeled 30 degrees, and BD is labeled 9. The segments AD and DC are indicated along the hypotenuse.
